coredata.dc:title: Developed ratiometric fluorescent probe as a logic platform for potential diagnosis of thyroid disease and diabetes and fluorescent ink
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xiaoting
Li, Ying
Zhang, Lei
coredata.dc:description: Iodine and glucose are indispensable substances whose abnormal levels are closely associated with a variety of diseases. Herein, CDs and RhB embedded Zr-metal-organic framework (RhB-CDs@MOF-808) as dual-emitting ratiometric fluorescent probe was fabricated via post-synthetic modification (PSM). The constructed ratiometric probe presents the emission at 450 nm of CDs was served as a reference to calibrate the emission at 575 nm attributed to RhB to achieve a remarkable self-calibration function. The fluorescence of probe is quenched by I3 − by virtue of inner filter effect (IFE), and the I3 − is reduced by glucose to achieve the recovery of fluorescence. In view of this, an analytical platform for the continuous detection of I3 − and glucose was established with wide linear ranges (I3 −: 0–220 μM; glucose: 0–180 μM) and low LODs (I3 − : 0.031 μM; glucose: 0.026 μM). The probe has been successfully exploited to the determination of iodine and glucose in real samples of food andbiology. More interestingly, an INHIBIT logic gate was established to provides a new platform for the preliminary diagnosis of thyroid disease and diabetes in clinical medicine. The probe as fluorescent ink achieved the continuous detection of iodine and glucose on a test paper expanding its potential application in samples containing iodine and glucose. This is the unprecedented attempt to exploit a ratiometric fluorescent probe for continuous detection of iodine and glucose, which can provide a new approach for the development of low-cost, sensitive and credible biological and clinical diagnostic thyroid disease and diabetes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Iodine
Glucose
Ratiometric fluorescent
Continuous detection
INHIBIT logic gate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The application of probe as fluorescent ink
Sensing performance of the ratiometric probe
The application of probe as fluorescent ink
The construction of an INHIBIT molecular logic platform

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC 51672116) and (NSFC52072164), Liaoning Province Science and Technology Planning Project (No. 20180551203) and Liao Ning Revitalization Talents Program (XLYC1902066).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images and diameter distribution of CDs; SEM images of MOF-808 (B) and RhB-CDs@MOF-808 (C); (D) UV − vis absorption spectra of MOF-808 (a)、CDs (b)、RhB (c) and RhB-CDS@MOF-808 (d); (E) XRD of simulated pattern (a), MOF-808 (b) and RhB-CDs@MOF-808 (c); (F) FT-IR spectra of MOF-808 (a), CDs (b), RhB (c) and RhB-CDs@MOF-808 (d).

(A) The emission spectra of RhB (a), CDs (b), MOF-808 (c), RhB@MOF-808 (d), CDs@MOF-808 (e) and RhB-CDs@MOF-808 (f). The illustration are photographs of RhB (a), CDs (b), and RhB-CDs@MOF-808 (c) solutions under ultraviolet light; (B) Fluorescence spectra of RhB-CDs@MOF-808 (a)、RhB-CDs@MOF-808-I3 − (b) and RhB-CDs@MOF-808-I3 −+glucose (c), The photographs of RhB-CDs@MOF-808 solution with continuous addition of I3 − and glucose under ultraviolet light; (C) Overlap between the absorption spectra of I3 − (a) and the excitation spectra of CDs (b) and RhB-CDS@MOF-808 (c); (D); Redox reaction between I3 − and glucose.

(A) Fluorescence response of the probe toward different potential interferences (blue bars), and the subsequent addition of I3 − (red bars); (B) Fluorescence response of the RhB-CDs@MOF-808-I3 − system toward different potential interferences (blue bars), and the subsequent addition of glucose (red bars). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the RhB-CDs@MOF-808 in the presence of different concentrations of I3 − (0–220 μM); (B) The linear relationship between (I575-I450)/(I575-I450)0 and the concentration of I3 −; (C) Fluorescence spectra of the RhB-CDs@MOF-808-I3 − system in the presence of different concentrations of glucose (0–180 μM); (D) The linear relationship between (I575-I450)/(I575-I450)0 and the concentration of glucose.

(A) The letter “E” changes to letter “F”, black area indicates the area overwritten with I3 − solution. The letter “F” changes back to letter “E” after this area overwritten with glucose; (B) The number “8” changes to number “5”, black area indicates the area overwritten with I3 − solution. The number “5” changes back to number “8” after this area overwritten with glucose. The letters and numbers were written with RhB-CDs@MOF-808 solution (20 μM). Scale bar: 0.5 cm. The images were taken under 365 nm UV light.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of probe in the presence of four input modes: (a) Probe; (b) Probe + I3 −; (c) Probe + glucose; (d) Probe-I3 − + glucose. (B) Output value of (I575-I450)/(I575-I450)0 (with a threshold of 0.3) under different inputs; (C) Blood glucose levels under different conditions; The principle of the initial diagnosis of thyroid disease and diabetes by the design of INHIBIT logic gate.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of CDs (A) and RhB-CDs@MOF-808) (B).
Analytical performance of reported fluorescence probes for iodine (or iodide) and glucose sensing.
Determination of I3 − in edible salt and human serum.
Determination of glucose in green tea beverage and human serum.

head.ce:title: Developed ratiometric fluorescent probe as a logic platform for potential diagnosis of thyroid disease and diabetes and fluorescent ink

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, 66 Chongshan Middle Road, Shenyang, Liaoning 110036, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, 66 Chongshan Middle Road, Shenyang, Liaoning 110036, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Iodine and glucose are indispensable substances whose abnormal levels are closely associated with a variety of diseases. Herein, CDs and RhB embedded Zr-metal-organic framework (RhB-CDs@MOF-808) as dual-emitting ratiometric fluorescent probe was fabricated via post-synthetic modification (PSM). The constructed ratiometric probe presents the emission at 450 nm of CDs was served as a reference to calibrate the emission at 575 nm attributed to RhB to achieve a remarkable self-calibration function. The fluorescence of probe is quenched by I3 − by virtue of inner filter effect (IFE), and the I3 − is reduced by glucose to achieve the recovery of fluorescence. In view of this, an analytical platform for the continuous detection of I3 − and glucose was established with wide linear ranges (I3 −: 0–220 μM; glucose: 0–180 μM) and low LODs (I3 − : 0.031 μM; glucose: 0.026 μM). The probe has been successfully exploited to the determination of iodine and glucose in real samples of food andbiology. More interestingly, an INHIBIT logic gate was established to provides a new platform for the preliminary diagnosis of thyroid disease and diabetes in clinical medicine. The probe as fluorescent ink achieved the continuous detection of iodine and glucose on a test paper expanding its potential application in samples containing iodine and glucose. This is the unprecedented attempt to exploit a ratiometric fluorescent probe for continuous detection of iodine and glucose, which can provide a new approach for the development of low-cost, sensitive and credible biological and clinical diagnostic thyroid disease and diabetes.

ce:section.ce:para: Physicalfitness has been a prerequisite for human survival forever. Iodine is an essential microelement for the production of thyroid hormone, which playsacrucialpartin regulating the process of growth, metabolic and brain development [1,2]. Inadequate iodine intake is prone to suffer from the iodine deficiency disorders (IDD) such as hypothyroidism, cognitivedisorder and cretinism [3,4]. However, excessive iodine intake may trigger simultaneous hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism, goiter or autoimmune thyroid disease and increased thyroid sensitivity to nuclear radiation [5,6]. The promptdetection of abnormal iodine levels can predict high-risk group prior to the occurrenceof subclinical or clinical thyroiddisease [7]. Therefore, real-time monitoring of iodine intake and controlling the iodine content in the body within an appropriaterange is of great significance for maintainingphysicalfitness. Glucose is also an indispensable substance for the human body. It is not only one of the main energy sources, but also participates in the metabolic process [8,9]. Glycometabolic disorder is associated with a variety of diseases, such as diabetes, cardiovascular disease, alzheimer's disease, etc [10]. Among them, diabetes bothering nearly 10% of the worldwide population and is a global health issues that can cause serious complications including of coma, epilepsy and organ damage [11]. Therefore, a flexible, sensitive, credible and economical method for the analysis of iodine and glucose is highly desired.

Fluorescence analytical method not only has the advantages of high sensitivity, real-time and simple operation, but also presents the merits of good selectivity, portability and reliable, etc., which is recognized as a superiorsensing detection technology [12,13]. Among them, the ratiometric fluorescent probe has drawn special attention because it overcomes the interference of instrument fluctuations, surrounding environment and the concentration of probe so as to improve the sensitivity and reliability of trace analytes [14,15], which is attributed to the self-calibration offered by measuring two (or more) emissions, rather than the absolute intensity of one emission [16]. A series of fluorescent conjugated polymers were developed to detect iodine, which enrich the work of fluorescence detection of iodine [17–19]. And copper nanoclusters (CuNCs@TA) [20] and MOFs material MIL-53(Al)-TDC [21] were used to detect iodine, respectively. In addition, CDs were synthesized and used for individual [22] or continuous detection of iodine [23]. With regard to glucose, most are detected by single-emitting probes [24–26], the ratiometric probes are still rare, such as CDs and Rh6G formed a ratiometric probe [27] and dual-emitting Eu-MOFs [10]. However, the continuous detection of iodine and glucose by ratiometric sensing technique has not been reported. If the ratiometric fluorescence method was developed to detect iodine and glucose continuously, the simple, accurate, continuous and specific detection can be achieved. Further, the detection based on the “turn on” mode of fluorescence can reduce background and improve signal to noise ratio (SNR) [28]. Simultaneous detection of iodine and glucose in a biological sample for the synchronous diagnosis of thyroid disease and diabetes has not been reported. On this basis, it is of great significance to establish a simple and robust dual-emitting fluorescent probe for the continuous detection of iodine and glucose.

Herein, a dual-emitting RhB-CDs@MOF-808 ratiometric fluorescent probe was successfully fabricated by introducing CDs and RhB into Zr-MOF-808 nanocages via PSM. The probe has been successfully exploited to the determination of iodine and glucose in real samples of food and biology. The continuous detection of I3 − and glucose by the probe as a fluorescent ink on the test paper indicates its potential application in the sample containing iodine and glucose. An INHIBIT logic gate was established to provide a new clinical platform for the preliminary diagnosis of thyroid disease and diabetes. Therefore, the excellent sensing performance of the probe provides a prospective application of ratiometric fluorescent probe in the biological and medical fields.

Tartaric acid, boric acid and arginine were from Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd; The acetone, anhydrous ethanol and rhodamine B (RhB) were obtained from Shenyang Laibo Electronics Co., Ltd. Glucose, Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4 were purchased from Sinopharm Shenyang Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All the experimental solutions were prepared with ultrapure water and all the chemicals used were of analytical grade.

The X-ray powder diffraction patterns of MOF-808 and RhB-CDs@MOF-808 were confirmed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8, Germany) with Cu kα radiation. The morphology CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-100SX, HITACHI). The morphologies of MOF-808 and RhB-CDs@MOF-808 were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi SU8000, Japan). The fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of products were analyzed with a spectrometer (Nicolet 5700, Thermo Fisher). The UV–vis spectrum was obtained on an ultraviolet absorption spectrophotometer (TU-1900, Puxi Analytic Instrument Ltd of Beijing, China). Fluorescence spectrum was measured from a fluorescence spectrometer (Varian Cary Eclipse 300, USA).

Preparation of CDs: The synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) based on the previous literature with a minor modification [29]. As shown in Scheme 1 A, tartaric acid (2.762 g), arginine (1.600 g) and boric acid (1.600 g) were dissolved in deionized water (60 mL). After stirring the mixture thoroughly for 15 min, the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave lined with 100 mL and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. After cooling down, light yellow transparent solution was obtained after filtered by 0.22 μm disposable filter membrane. The filtrate was precipitated with acetone and the obtained white emulsion precipitate was washed with methanol/acetone mixed solution for three times, the final product was vacuumdried at 60 ℃ for 12 h to obtain light yellow powder.

Preparation of RhB-CDs@MOF-808: The synthesis of MOF-808 is identical to the work we reported earlier [30]. The synthesis route of RhB-CDs@MOF-808 composite material was shown in Scheme 1B, 200 mg MOF-808 and 100 mg CDs were added into 60 mL ethanol solution of rhodamine B (RhB) solution (0.002 M) under stirring, and reflux stirring was carried out at 80 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature and centrifugation, washed with anhydrous ethanol until the supernate is colorless, and the resulting product was dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

Sensing process of I3 −: The detection of I3 − was carried out in phosphate buffer saline (PBS, 0.02 M, pH 7.4) at room temperature. During a typical experimental procedure, 10 mg RhB-CDs@MOF-808 was dispersed in 50 mL PBS buffer solution under ultrasonic conditions for 5 min to form a homogeneous reserve solution. Subsequently, 200 μL I3 − solution of different concentrations and 800 μL PBS were added to the 2 mL reserve solution, the obtained solution was mixed for 30 s before fluorescence measurement was conducted with excitation at 300 nm.

Sensing process of glucose: In order to determine the concentration of glucose, I3 − solution (200 μM) was introduced to RhB-CDs@MOF-808 (100 μL, 200 mg/L) to form a fluorescence quenching system of RhB-CDs@MOF-808-I3 − as a glucose detection platform. And 200 μL glucose solutions of different concentration were added and incubated for 30 min at room temperature, respectively. The mixtures with a total volume of 3 mL were shaken and poured into a cuvette before fluorescence recovery spectra were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 300 nm.

Implementation of interference experiments: In the case of I3 − and glucose were selected as analytes, some potential co-existing interferers include common ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, NH4 +, Ba2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Co2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Cl−, Br−, F−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, SO4 2− and CH3COO−), amino acids (glycine, L-arginine, and L-tyrosine), sugars (glucose, sucrose, and starch), and food additives (sodium citrate, sodium benzoate, sodium tartrate, cyclodextrin, and chitosan) have been studied as co-interferences. The selective experiment of probe to I3 − and RhB-CDs@MOF-808-I3 − system to glucose was evaluated by adding some other interference substances into the solutions, and the other detection processes are similar to sensing process of I3 − and glucose.

The healthy human serum samples were obtained from Liaoning University Hospital, and all experiments were carried out according to the relevant laws and regulations. 40 μL of serum sample was diluted 50-fold for the analysis of iodine and glucose.Green tea beverage was diluted 150-fold for glucose detection. The iodized salt sold in the market was ground into powder and prepared into solution for iodine detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of probe as fluorescent ink
ce:section.ce:para: The common filter paper was cut into the shape of 8 cm × 8 cm. The letters and numbers were written with RhB-CDs@MOF-808 solution (20 μM). Scale bar: 0.5 cm. The images were taken under 365 nm UV light. The fluorescence quenching was completed about 30 s and recovery processes were obtained within 30 min.

The morphology of the synthesized CDs was characterized by TEM (Fig. 1 A, it can be observed that the CDs are near-spherical in shape with good dispersion. The diameter distribution shows that the sizes are within the range of 2–11 nm. The morphologies of MOF-808 and RhB-CDs@MOF-808 were observed by SEM (Fig. 1B, C, MOF-808 are octahedral nanoparticles with average diameter about 60 nm, the morphology of RhB-CDs@MOF-808 is almost the same as that of MOF-808.The results indicate that the morphology and size of MOF-808 insignificantly change after the loading of RhB and CDs.

As shown in Fig. 1D, RhB shows the intense absorption peak at 553 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectra, but RhB-CDs@MOF-808 (d) has a weak even negligible absorption peak at 553 nm compared with MOF-808 (a), the results confirmed that CDs and RhB were incorporated into MOF-808. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern characterized the structure of MOF-808 before and after adding RhB and CDs (Fig. 1E, the diffraction peaks of MOF-808 (b) and RhB-CDs@MOF-808 (c) matched well with the simulated pattern (a). The results confirmed that MOF-808 is high pure phase product, and the crystal structure of MOF-808 is invariable after encapsulation of RhB and CDs.

The formation and functional groups of the products were analyzed by FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 1F, the absorption peak of RhB-CDs@MOF-808 (d) at 650 cm−1 originated from the formation of Zr-O bond of MOF-808 (a). The absorption peaks of RhB-CDs@MOF-808 (d) at 3212 cm−1, 3426 cm−1, 1740 cm−1 and 1092 cm−1 are consistent with those of CDs (b) [29]. The absorption peak of RhB (c) was not observed in RhB-CDs@MOF-808 (d) is probably to the small content of RhB. The above results indicate that RhB and CDs are successfully loaded on the basis of MOF-808 [31–33].

The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra were recorded, as shown in Fig. 2 A. Compared with MOF-808 (c), RhB-CDs@MOF-808 (f) possess two distinct fluorescence emission peaks at 450 nm and 575 nm, which are attributed to CDs and RhB, respectively. It was found that the emission peak of RhB-CDs@MOF-808 (f) solution at 575 nm was observably higher than that of pure RhB (a) solution, which is due to the non-radiation energy transfer existing in the aggregated dye molecules and resulting in the fluorescence quenching of the RhB [34–35]. As shown in Fig. S1, solid state RhB has almost no fluorescence due to aggregation induced quenching effect.However, the RhB embedded in MOF-808 with good dispersion so as to this phenomenon is inhibited under the superior structure of MOFs encapsulation [36–37]. MOF-808 encapsulation presents an enhancement on the fluorescence of RhB, which can be used to construct a highly sensitive analytical platform. The characteristic peak of CDs in RhB-CDs@MOF-808 (f) is lower than that of single CDs (b), which is attributed to the competitive excitation energy between RhB and CDs, resulting in the decrease of emission intensity of CDs. It can be observed that there is an intense blue fluorescence of prepared CDs under UV light and the fluorescence of RhB-CDs@MOF-808 is orange, which is due to the dominant strong emission of RhB. At the same time, it indicating the dual-emitting RhB-CDs@MOF-808 fluorescent composite material has been successfully prepared.

As shown in Fig. 2B, there are two strong emission peaks at 450 nm and 575 nm in fluorescence spectra of RhB-CDs@MOF-808 (a). However, the two emission intensities are extremely low in the present of I3 − (b). After the addition of glucose (c), the fluorescence intensity of the two peaks recovered significantly. As can be seen illustration in Fig. 2B, upon adding I3 − to the solution of RhB-CDs@MOF-808, the orange fluorescence (a) rapidly quenched and darkened (b) under UV light. Whereafter, the orange fluorescence was restored and strengthened (c) while adding glucose to the system. Therefore, I3 − and glucose play the role in tuning fluorescence “off” and “on” in the total sensing process, respectively.

It can be observed from Fig. 2B and Fig. S2A that the fluorescence intensity of RhB-CDs@MOF-808 significantly decreased after the addition of I3 − solution. For the sake of study this phenomenon and the implementation of the subsequent work. As shown in Fig. 2C, we measured the UV–vis absorption spectra of I3 − (a) and found that it overlapped with the excitation spectra of RhB-CDs@MOF-808 (b). It indicating the existence of inner-filter effect (IFE) [38], the excitation energy is effectively absorbed by the I3 − resulting in the dramatically quenching the fluorescence of the probe [36,39]. According to Fig. 2B and Fig. S2B, the fluorescence of the RhB-CDs@MOF-808-I3 − system recovered after the addition of glucose. However, it can be seen from Fig. S2C that in the context of only glucose was present in RhB-CDS@MOF-808 solutions, the fluorescence spectra were consistent with that of probe. It is confirmed the absent of influence of glucose to fluorescence of RhB-CDS@MOF-808 probe. To the best of our knowledge, a redox reaction can occur between glucose and I3 −, as shown in Fig. 2D. Glucose can reduce I3 − to I−, and the reductive product I− was verified to have a negligible effect on the fluorescence of RhB-CDs@MOF-808 (Fig. S2D. It can be manifested that the I3 − is reduced by glucose to achieve the recovery of the fluorescence. Therefore, a fluorescence “turn on” analytical platform for glucose was successfully constructed based on the redox reaction between glucose and I3 −.

The fluorescence stability (Fig. S3A, pH (Fig. S3B, reaction time (Fig. S3C, Fig. S3D and the concentration of I3 − (Fig. S4 in the detection process was investigated, which were provided in supplementary materials. In order to obtain the optimum experimental results, it has been confirmed thatRhB-CDs@MOF-808 possesses excellent fluorescence stability, and pH of 7.4, response time of 30 s for I3 − and 30 min for glucose and the concentration of I3 − of 200 μM were selected for the following study.

The requirement of high selectivity is also indispensable in most circumstances, especially in practical application. Therefore, underthe circumstanceofI3 − as the analyte, some different interfering substancespotentially present in edible salt and human serum were investigated to fluorescent response at the concentration of 100 × 10−4 mol/L (Fig. 3 A. It was observed that they had negligible influence on the fluorescence intensity ratio (I575-I450)/(I575-I450)0. But the fluorescence of the system variated obviously after adding 180 μM I3 −, and the interferences have little effect on the detection of it, indicating that the probe has a good selectivity for I3 −.

In the condition of glucose as the analyte, some different substances might be present in beverage and the human serum were studied at the concentration of (100 × 10−4 mol/L). As shown in Fig. 3B. The results indicate that all interfering substances had no impact on the fluorescence of RhB-CDs@MOF-808-I3 − system. Subsequently, the (I575-I450)/(I575-I450)0 was recovered to almost the original level after adding 200 μM glucose to the above systems, while the other interfering substances had little influence on the fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing performance of the ratiometric probe
ce:section.ce:para: The two emission peaks of RhB-CDs@MOF-808 composite differ visibly in the aspects of peak intensities and locations. The fluorescence intensity at 575 nm is about 10 times that at 450 nm and the two emissions are without overlap.It is worth noting that the fluorescence intensity of the two emission peaks showed different quenching extents after the addition of I3 −. The blue fluorescence intensity decreased slightly at 450 nm, while the orange fluorescence intensity at 575 nm dramatically quenched. On the basis of these fluorescence fluctuation features, the weak emission at 450 nm can be regarded as a reference to calibrate the strong emission signal at 575 nm. Hence, the relative fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe was used instead of its absolute intensity as the response signal to sensing analytes. The expression ofthe relative fluorescence intensity is shown in Fig. S5. According to the above discussion, the relative fluorescence intensity of RhB-CDs@MOF-808 solution is referred as (I575-I450)0, and it is referred as I575-I450 after adding the analyte. Therefore,(I575-I450)/(I575-I450)0 was introduced to evaluate the fluorescence response effect of adding different analytes.

According to the previous discussion, Fluorescence quantitative titration was first conducted to determine I3 − in the solution by quenching the fluorescence of RhB-CDs@MOF-808 under the optimal experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually with the increase of the concentrations of I3 −, and the linear relationship of (I575-I450)/(I575-I450)0 with respect to the concentrations of I3 − in the range of 0–220 μM was obtained (Fig. 4B. The linear equation is y = -0.00399x + 1.0101 (R2 = 0.9986). It indicating that the established analytical method for detecting I3 − not only presents a good linear relationship, but also has a wide linear range. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as the following formula: LOD = 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation (n = 9) of the continuous determination of blank RhB-CDs@MOF-808 solution for 9 times, k is the slope of the standard curve, so the obtained LOD was 0.031 μM. The result shows that the proposed detection method has a high sensitivity for detecting I3 −.

Subsequently, we utilizing RhB-CDs@MOF-808-I3 − fluorescence quenching system to construct glucose sensing platform. As shown in Fig. 4C, under the optimal experimental conditions, the fluorescence intensity gradually recover with the increase of the concentrations of glucose. At the same time, the linear relationship between (I575-I450)/(I575-I450)0 and concentration of glucose was obtained in the range of 0–180 μM (Fig. 4D. The linear equation is y = 0.0048x + 0.1634 (R2 = 0.9959), achieving the LOD is 0.026 μM. It confirming that the constructed platform for sensing glucose in RhB-CDs@MOF-808-I3 − system not only holds a wide linear range, but also has a high sensitivity. The above results confirm that the prepared ratiometric probe presents a desirable performance for sensitive and accurate determination of I3 − and glucose. And the probe not only could be an ideal detector for the I3 − and glucose, but also may be comparable to or even better thanthe previous reported probes (Table 1 .

In order to verify the detection performance of the established method in practical application. Serum iodine is an important biomarker of iodine metabolism reflecting recent iodine intake. Therefore, RhB-CDs@MOF-808 probe was served for the determination of iodine in edible salt and human serum. And the detection of glucose in beverage and human serum were carried out. The experimental results were shown in Tables 2 and 3
. The recoveries were calculated to be in the range of 94.0%-109.0%, and the relative standard deviations (RSD, n = 3) were 2.4%-3.8%, indicating that this analytical method is accurate and precise for the detection of iodine and glucose in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of probe as fluorescent ink
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the unique fluorescence “off–on” properties and the selective recognition of I3 − and glucose, we anticipate that the luminescent materials with special fluorescence properties might enable detecting I3 − and glucose on a test paper. The prepared fluorescent probe could be developed as a fluorescent ink and written on paper. As shown in Fig. 5 A, we wrote the letter “E” on the common filter paper with RhB-CDs@MOF-808 solution (20 μM) in the first, the letter “E” changes to letter “F” after the area overwritten with I3 − solution. Interestingly, the letter “F” changes back to letter “E” after this area overwritten with glucose. Similarly, I3 − ions to intentionally hide area of the number “8” on the filter paper (Fig. 5B to present “5”. The number “5” recovered to number “8” after this area was treated with glucose. The fluorescence quenching was completed about 30 s and recovery processes is obtained within 30 min. Fluorescence quenching and recovery were achieved synchronously by detecting I3 − and glucose on the test paper. Therefore, the method is convenient and presents a wide application prospect in the detection of iodine and glucose in food, beverages, medicine, etc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The construction of an INHIBIT molecular logic platform
ce:section.ce:para: On account of the above discussion about fluorescence response features, a characteristic INHIBIT logic gate of RhB-CDs@MOF-808 probe was constructed by building two input signals of I3 − (Input1) and glucose (Input2), and the (I575-I450)/(I575-I450)0 values of the two emission peaks were served as the output signals. The presence and absence of two inputs I3 − and glucose can be defined as “1″ and “0” states. The (I575-I450)/(I575-I450)0 value corresponding to 0.3 was set as the threshold, and the output value below the threshold level (0.3) is converted to symbol “0”, while the output value above the threshold is corresponding to symbol “1”. As shown in Fig. 6 A and B, when (Input1, Input2) is (1,0), the output of (I575-I450)/(I575-I450)0 is significantly lower than the threshold of 0.3 (Output = 0). On the contrary, the values of (I575-I450)/(I575-I450)0 maintain higher than the threshold level (Output = 1) when (Input1, Input2) is (0,0), (0,1) or (1,1), respectively. Previous studies have already reported that the serum iodine is an important biological indicator of iodine metabolism reflecting recent iodine intake, the levels of serum iodine in euthyroid adults in iodine adequate, sufficient and excess content were 0.37–0.83, 0.38–0.98, 0.46–1.01 and 0.79–1.02 μmol/L, respectively. And high levels of serum iodine (>0.79 μmol/L) may be a risk factor for thyroid diseases [45] (Fig. 6C (a)). Based on the above data, the detection limit (LOD) of RhB-CDs@MOF-808 for glucose was 0.031 μmol/L which is low enough to meet the tentative diagnosis of thyroid disease. As shown in Fig. 6D, through the output value of the logic platform, the I3 − level can be obtained to estimate whether it is suffering from thyroid disease. Normally, the fasting blood glucose (FBG) level is 3.9–6.1 mmol/L, 1-hour postprandial blood glucose (1hPG) is 6.7–9.4 mmol/L, 2-hour postprandial blood glucose (2hPG) is not higher than 7.8 mmol/L. But in diabetic patient, the fasting blood glucose (FBG) level is higher than 7.1 mmol/L, 2-hour postprandial blood glucose (2hPG) is higher than 11.1 mmol/L [46–47] (Fig. 6C (b)). Similarly, the detection limit (LOD) of the probe for glucose was 0.026 μmol/L which is also low enough to fit the initial diagnosis of diabetes. As shown in Fig. 6C (c), whether it is suffering from diabetes is determined by the blood glucose level of the output value of the logic platform. This fluorescence INHIBIT logic platform provides a new platform for the preliminary diagnosis of thyroid disease and diabetes in clinical medicine.

A dual-emitting ratiometric fluorescent probe RhB-CDs@MOF-808 was fabricated via post-synthetic modification. The method has been successfully developed to the determination of iodine and glucose in real samples of food andbiology. An INHIBIT logic gate was established to provides a new platform for the preliminary diagnosis of diabetes in clinical medicine. The probe as fluorescent ink achieved continuous detecting I3 − and glucose on a test paper indicating its potential application in samples containing iodine and glucose. The mechanism of “off–on”the RhB-CDs@MOF-808 fluorescence response was also discussed in detail. The prepared RhB-CDs@MOF-808 probe can realize the accurate, continuous, specific and highly sensitive detection of iodine and glucose. Hence, a simple, sensitive and reliable fluorescence probe was exploited in biomedicine and food qualitysupervision.

Xiaoting Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Ying Li: Data curation, Investigation. Lei Zhang: Resources, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thisprojectwassupportedbythe National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC 51672116) and (NSFC52072164), Liaoning Province Science and Technology Planning Project(No.20180551203) andLiao Ning Revitalization Talents Program(XLYC1902066).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106879.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
